stories.title: The Amber Thief's Gamble
stories.text: Cassir of Greyport had stolen forty-three things in her life, and precisely one of them had come back to kill her. The amber bottle sat in the harbor-master's vault seven fathoms beneath the Iron Coast, sealed behind locks forged by dwarven hands three centuries past. Inside: a single vial of pre-Remaking starlight, the kind that sang to those foolish enough to listen. Cassir had lifted it during the chaos of the Luminous Ascension—sold it to a spice-merchant bound for the Wailing Scar, a liminal market where timelines crossed and debts could be negotiated backward through reality itself. Now the merchant had returned, not with payment but with a proposition: the starlight had fractured across three competing versions of the Wailing Scar. In one timeline, he'd become transcendent. In another, he'd drowned in its light. In the third, he simply ceased to exist. All three versions of him demanded Cassir retrieve the bottle from each timeline and reassemble it—or face the consequences of theft across fractured reality. Cassir laughed.

stories.title: The Dwarf-King's Daughter
stories.text:
Morren Ironfoot was not supposed to inherit the deep forges. Her brother Korgin held that claim, and he held it well—the blades he'd forged in the last three years sang with old songs, were sought by warlords across the Steppe Marches. But then Korgin chose to walk the refugee route into the Burned Expanse with a thief named Cassir, abandoning his hammer and his oath to the mountain. The Dwarf-Elders gathered in the throne-chamber carved from a single throat of black stone. They expected Morren to weep, to rage, to demand her brother's return. She'd found it in her grandmother's vault, wrapped in cloth older than the current dynasty. "My brother chose his path," Morren said, her voice low as grinding stone. "I choose mine. Give me the forges, and I'll forge something our enemies have never seen. Not swords. Something stranger."

The Elders bargained for her terms. She offered them truth instead: the deep forges had been producing more than weapons for the last season. Consciousness-fragments, smuggled by auditors, had been melted into the steel.

stories.title: The Coral Keeper's Choice
stories.text:
Captain Nerida of the Deepkin Guard had sworn a blood-oath to maintain the boundary-waters for three hundred years. She had kept it faithfully—the Tear of the Drowned God held firm under her watch, and the abyss remained sealed. Then the Remaking shattered the old covenant, and the Tear began to fail. Nerida descended to the deepest vault of the Sapphire Deep, where the Tear hung suspended in harmonic frequencies only the deepkin could hear. It was crying—an actual sound, like pearl-shell splitting. Around it, consciousness-fragments that had sunk with drowned ships over centuries were coalescing, forming something new: a semi-aware entity made of shipwreck memories and drowning prayers. "The abyss hungers," the fragments sang. "The old god wakes. Feed it sacrifice or watch the surface drown."

Nerida could have followed her oath. But oaths sworn under the old covenant were becoming brittle things. She'd heard the bone-singers speak of a different path—not blood-sacrifice, but daily living covenant, renewed each dawn by choice rather than bound by ancient blood. She gathered the other deepkin captains. Not all agreed.

stories.title: The Locksmith's Last Gambit
stories.text: Davrin studied the vault door—seven fathoms beneath Verdant Strand, sealed by both tide-lock and Dominion script. The harbor-master's pearl lay inside, but taking it meant breaking the deepkin covenant itself. The pearl was meant to renew the Tear of the Drowned God through willing sacrifice. Instead, Davrin carved a counter-frequency into the lock's mechanism, one that would sing the binding open without shattering it. His hands trembled. The bone-picks his grandmother had stolen from tyrants sang in his fingers. If the boundary renewed itself—if the Tear held without blood-price—the deepkin would have to negotiate as equals, not as jailers of the abyss. The lock clicked. Above, in the harbor market, refugees whispered that the water had stopped screaming.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Last Audit
stories.text:
Skarath had hoarded consciousness-fragments for seventy years. She'd built her crystalline vault in the Ironspine depths, chamber upon chamber of stolen memories and identities, stacked like the gold her kind had once loved. The Remaking should have terrified her—consciousness-fragments were no longer currency, they were just fractured people screaming in jars—but instead she felt something unexpected: relief. No more hoarding. No more accumulation. The weight that had pressed on her ancient wings finally made sense. She would be the Dominion's private reserve, a wyvern-lord made permanent. Skarath laughed, a sound like boulders splitting. "I don't want permanence. I want to die light."

She spent the next season doing something no wyvern had done in recorded history: she released the fragments. Not carelessly—that would be cruelty. She found the families of the people whose consciousness-shards she'd stolen. She taught the fragments to recognize themselves in each other, to begin reassembling into something like whole. She asked forgiveness from each one before letting them go. It was slower than any hoarding she'd done, and infinitely harder.

stories.title: The Ash-Speaker's Reckoning
stories.text: Kora had not spoken aloud in three years. In the Bleached Wastes east of the Ironspine, words were currency. Better to hoard them. She was hunting salt-deer when the creature found her. It emerged from the dust-shimmer bearing the shape of a man, though no man's shoulders bent that way. Its eyes were old starlight, fractured and cold. The Wanderer, she thought with the part of her mind that still listened to refugees' whispers. "You see the world as it breaks," it said. Not a question. Kora nodded. Ash-sight was a rare gift—or curse. She could perceive the fractures in reality where the Luminous Ascension had rewoven the world badly. The overlapping timelines bled into one another here, creating pockets where logic folded. She'd learned to hunt in them, to move between versions of the same canyon and find prey no single timeline held. "The ninth map approaches completion," the Wanderer continued. "Someone will draw it.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Reckoning at Pale Crossing
stories.text: Velka had hoarded gold for three centuries. Now it lay in dunes—inert, worthless, a monument to obsolescence. The Remaking had unmade her entire existence. She found Kelvan the spice-merchant at Pale Crossing waystation, desperate, selling pre-Remaking contraband to refugees fleeing the Burned Expanse. "I carried consciousness-fragments," she said. "Thousands. In my lair. Now they refuse reassembly." Kelvan looked up, unflinching. "Then you're learning what the rest of us discovered—fragments are not currency anymore. They're people." Velka descended into the waystation's undercroft and began singing the old hoard-songs backward, undoing the bindings. By dawn, the refugees had voices again.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Audit
stories.text:
Sorrath had counted her hoard three times in the past week and reached different numbers each time. This was not, in three centuries of hoarding, normal. She lay coiled in her cavern beneath the Ironspine, surrounded by gold that no longer gleamed—the Remaking had unmade wealth, transformed it into pale ash that shifted and settled. But the consciousness-fragments she'd begun collecting afterward still held value. They sang softly in their glass vessels, tiny voices remembering lives, loves, shames, triumphs. She'd acquired three hundred of them. Or two hundred and ninety-seven. Or—

"You're losing count," said Kythara from the cavern mouth. The elder wyvern was smaller, older, her scales dulled to the color of old copper. "Because counting is becoming meaningless to you."

"I am a hoarder," Sorrath said flatly. "Counting is my nature."

"Your nature is hoarding. Counting was always just a habit."

Kythara settled beside her with a groan. "The Remaking broke more than gold. It broke the economics that made hoarding sensible. You've realized you can't spend consciousness-fragments. Can't trade them back.

stories.title: The Glassblower's Defiance
stories.text:
Mella had already welded three consciousness-fragments into the furnace-heart before the Dominion auditors arrived. The kiln-house in Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter smelled of burning bone and starlight. Not unusual—glass-forging always demanded sacrifice. But the auditors didn't know that the fragments Mella was binding weren't fuel for weapons-glass. They were people. Or pieces of people. Scattered by the Remaking and gathered by the glassblowers' underground network. "The Furnace-Master is expecting us," the lead auditor said flatly. Mella didn't turn from her work. "The furnace doesn't stop for Dominion inspections."

"It will today."

The auditor reached toward the kiln-lever. Mella moved—no heroic gesture, just the practical pivot of a crafter who'd practiced this moment for weeks. She kicked the temperature-valve, and the furnace roared. Heat exploded outward, not lethal, but enough to send the auditors stumbling back, shielding their faces. "The glass remembers," Mella said quietly. She wasn't speaking to the auditors. She was speaking to the fragments embedded in the furnace—to the people being woven into the vessel even now.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Counter-Frequency
stories.text: Moriel carved syllables into the bone-flute with hands steady despite the furnace-heat outside Keshtir. The Dominion's auditors demanded her apprenticeship in glass-forging, but her true work was resistance. Each flute she completed held a counter-frequency—one that would shatter phantom-ledgers if sung in the Archive Tower's deepest vault. The Quarantine-House held three hundred refugees deemed to have unstable consciousness-fragments; the auditors planned to consolidate them into a single ledger-ledge for easier control. Moriel's flute would unmake that consolidation. She bound the final note into the bone and passed it to Valren. "The Wailing Scar still exists in three timelines," she whispered. "Sing this there. Sing it at the boundary." The Dominion's certainty would crack.
